records.ety: [F. redonder, L. redundare; pref. red-, re-, re- + undare to rise in waves or surges, fr. unda a wave. See Undulate, and cf. Redundant.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Redounded; p. pr. & vb. n. Redounding.]

defs: To roll back, as a wave or flood; to be sent or driven back; to flow back, as a consequence or effect; to conduce; to contribute; to result.
quotes: The evil, soon Driven back, redounded as a flood on those From whom it sprung.
The honor done to our religion ultimately redounds to God, the author of it.
both . . . will devour great quantities of paper, there will no small use redound from them to that manufacture.

defs: To be in excess; to remain over and above; to be redundant; to overflow.
quotes: For every dram of honey therein found, A pound of gall doth over it redound.

defs: The coming back, as of consequence or effect; result; return; requital.
quotes: We give you welcome; not without redound Of use and glory to yourselves ye come.
